coins.obverse_description: [Engelhard ‘E’ Logo in Octagon]  [999+]  [FINE SILVER]  [100 TR OZ]  [Serial Number]

coins.notes:
Commentary: All Engelhard is grateful to have sourced this example’s history from the original owner of bar A0006.  This 100oz Manfra, Tordella & Brooks bar was produced by Engelhard in 1986 for MTB Bank, New York.  We believe these examples were originally issued with an assay certificate, which typically demands a higher valuation when present.  It is speculated these bars were offered to customers who opened a new account at MTB Bank, New York, with $100,000.00 deposit.  We now have 15 serial numbers logged and remain confident in our estimate of <25 examples on this variety, as these were very exclusive and beautiful bars and not likely melted.  This ‘A’ prefix serial range is shared with MTB bars in 5oz and 10oz class.  Bar A0454 sold privately on January 20, 2017 for $4,000.00.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 1st series in 6-digit serial variation, three zero suffix on weight designation.  We believe this serial number range spans from approximately 010000 to 024800.  Anomalies include occasional triangular stamping to left of serial number and a separately serial numbered ingot, 52937, both pictured.  Note the triangular stamping is the bottom half of the leading ‘X’ filler blank that slipped out of position.  We believe the early assignments in this serial number range were shared with the error ‘X’ and ‘Y’ bars in 10oz class of same approximate production period.  The only known example below is unique in that the serial number gang stamp was misaligned, requiring Engelhard to hand stamp the last two digits individually.
Ingot 013622, presented below, demonstrates a unique serial stamping error.
Some ingots demonstrate a bubbled obverse finish, resulting in a very light serial number stamping, such as ingot 015933, pictured below,  Our thanks to Sandy Taylor for this contribution!

coins.notes:
Commentary: 8th series in ‘W’ serial prefix, produced exclusively at the ‘Engelhard West’ recycling facility operated in Anaheim, California for a 21-year run from 1979-2000.  There is strong evidence that supports production timeframe overlapped on both ‘P’ and ‘W’ series ingots with the ‘W’ prefix beginning at ‘W050XXX’.  Some of the early ingots were apparently poured into cold casting moulds that caused irregularities on the reverse and edges.  However, this process did not result in weight deficit.  Many examples like W118483 shown at left illustrate beautiful cooling lines and surface colors as a result of the production process.  Note that ingot W050552 demonstrates a serial number font error as demonstrated below.

coins.notes:
Commentary: Specialty collector bar produced for Manfra, Tordella & Brookes, 1986 mintage. It is important to note that the Metal Arts Company [MA] produced the first 10oz bars for MTB, serial range 1 to 5,000. JM produced them in wide and narrow formats. All Engelhard estimates serial range 6,500 to 16,000 and there appears to be a 1,500 serial number gap between MA, JM and Engelhard production runs. Occasionally found with original assay certificate, which typically demands a higher valuation when present.  Some bars were included in a 1oz, 5oz & 10oz boxed commemorative set with estimated mintage of 1,000 total sets, pictured below.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 3rd series in 6-digit serial variation with a number ‘8’ leading prefix. This variety demonstrates a ‘^’ over-stamp to the left of serial number. The serial number errors on these pieces resulted from part of the letter ‘X’ that was used as a filler for the lead position on the rotary stamp. This leading filler ‘X’ slipped out of position just enough to leave an impression of the bottom or top half of the letter. Also note the strength of the impression left by leading number ‘8’ due to its lack of use. We have registry entries noted for these error pieces in very tight ranges between 811855 to ‘8’14487 to include the unique ‘Y’ prefix variety such as ‘Y’12590 and believe that this range was shared with the 100oz examples with zero prefix. We estimate partial ‘X’ ‘8’ error stamp variety mintage at <250. Ingot 811864 sold privately on February 20, 2017 for $1,200.00 and again on MinEx auction on 6/18/23 for $1,567.50. Ingot 811882 sold on MinEx auction on 2/19/23 for $2719.20 final sale price.  Tulasi LoPriore’s example 13436, presented below, demonstrates the lack of serial prefix digit ‘8’ and includes the unique double ‘X’ impression.
Additionally, ingot 10473 demonstrates the same obverse double ‘X’ impression, but also includes 5th series 5oz ingot stampings on the reverse as presented below.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 3rd series in 5-digit serial variation with ‘MFR’ [Metals Fabrication & Refining] designation. Analysis indicates these were produced in six specific serial runs of 36xxx, 48xxx, 55xxx, 59xxx, 65xxx, 74xxx and have estimated mintage of less than 50 total examples on each run. It is important to note that ‘MFR’ is not a serial number suffix as some collectors may claim. The 100oz class examples confirm this as their serial number and ‘MFR’ designation are stamped on opposed corners of the ingot. Ingot 48283 ‘MFR’ sold via eBay auction for $1481.99 on July 6, 2021.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 7th series in ‘P’ serial prefix variation, these examples demonstrate tapered leading edges [side slanting inward from top to bottom] so that the top has more surface area than the bottom. Note the space in ’99 9+’ purity designation. Some early 10th series bars were assigned serial numbers within the high serial range of 7th series during production transition, such as P076324. We believe complete transition from 7th series to standard production 10th series occurred close to serial number P076654. Note the bottom photo shows this variety with a letter ‘K’ below the blank serial prefix stamp. While the Waffle Back pattern was common on these production pieces after serial assignment P010000, it is important to note that there are production anomalies on these examples due to backfilling of serial number assignment. As such, it is not uncommon for a regular cast finish reverse to be observed on an ingot in serial range beyond the approximate estimated staring point of P010000.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 9th series in ‘W’ serial prefix variation, produced exclusively at the ‘Engelhard West’ recycling facility operated in Anaheim, California in operation from 1979-1981+/- .  It is possible that production timeframe overlapped on both ‘W’ and ‘P’ series ingots. NOTE: Between W009073 – W009659 the production process changed from grinding excess weight from edges to removing from obverse. We believe this practice was exclusive to the Anaheim recycling facility and have included photos of an early and later production example for comparison as well as two ingots close to this production transition. Estimated serial number range W000001 to W017999.  It is interesting to note the early production examples of this variety are of a wider casting mould, as demonstrated in the photo below.  It appears that this unique casting mould was limited to perhaps the first 75 ingots produced, spanning from serial number W000001 through approximately W000075.  Ingot W000066 sold via eBay auction for $1,195.00 on January 19, 2019.  Ingot W000085 sold privately for $1,200.00 on June 12, 2018.
Ingot W012296, pictured below, demonstrates both obverse surface grinding of excess weight and a linen pattern reverse similar to Canadian 10oz and 20oz ingots.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 10th series in ‘P’ serial prefix variation, last variety in truly old-poured form. Note the reverse of these examples occasionally demonstrate a ‘reverse’ convex stamping impression of the top line found on the observe. We have included multiple photos to demonstrate reverse pattern variations and believe this was caused by an inadvertent stamping of the production line surface when a bar was not present. The stamping force altered the production line surface area in such a way that the hex-screw head pattern also began leaving an impression. Some early 10th series bars were assigned serial numbers within the high serial range of 7th series during production transition, such as P076324. We believe complete transition from 7th series to standard production 10th series occurred close to serial number P076654. This ‘P’ prefix serial run was also more commonly shared with 11th and 12th series 10oz ‘P’ class, 8th series 100oz extruded class and 1st series portrait ‘Tall E’ extruded class.  Note the double-stamped second number ‘1’ in the photo below of ingot P167134.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 1st series ingot in Canadian type ‘A’ variation. The placement of this ingot’s weight/purity stampings and casting dimension are familiar to its 2nd series United States counterpart. Canadian ingots across all weight class are typically observed in 6-digit serial format with purity stamping on left and weight class on right. The initial run of this variety was in a rare 5-digit serial assignment. Based on this ingot’s dimension, we speculate Canada was initially provided with casting moulds used by the United States 3rd series 5-digit ingots at that time for this 1st series of Canadian ingots, as this 1st series also demonstrates the more rounded edges, unlike the 2nd series. Interesting to note the serial font on these ingots are exclusive to this variety, specifically the ‘1’ and ‘3’ numbers.  Ingot 85808 sold on December 19, 2022 on eBay for $1,700.00.

coins.obverse_description: [999+] [10.000] [Bull Hallmark in Elongated Octagon] [Serial Number]

coins.notes:
Commentary: 4th series ingot in Canadian variation. Canada-specific casting mould and first variety to include ‘floating decimal’ on weight class as well as new font for purity 9′s common to Canadian production. This series also includes a new hallmark with larger letters within elongated octagon and a smaller center ‘dot’ on the Bull Logo. Although most examples studied demonstrated Canadian serial number font with occasional United States serial font variation, we believe this series ingot to be the first truly ‘All Canadian’ 10oz variety. These ingots are typically seen with the purity stamp directly above the ‘Bull Logo’ and the weight stamp further to the right. Known examples include 172xxx, 187xxx, 188xxx and 194xxx serial range. Approximate dimensions of 4th series variety are 2 ½” x 1 7/16″ x ½”.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 4th series in ‘MFR’ [Metals Fabrication & Refining] designation.  It is important to note that ‘MFR’ is not a serial number suffix as some collectors may claim.  The 100oz class examples confirm this as their serial number and ‘MFR’ designation are stamped on opposed corners of the ingot.  We believe there were intermittent overflow serial assignments with non ‘MFR’ designated ingots and that serial range is not indicative of mintage.  Ingot 54167 ‘MFR’ sold via eBay auction on 7/30/23 for $810.11, and it was a marginal example with some surface scratches.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 7th series standard ‘T.OZ’ production ingot.  A leading ‘zero’ was added in the 059300 range that continued through end of production and the design was changed shortly thereafter.  All varieties are pictured for reference and, based on known serial numbers, we believe serial numbers span from 55500 to 061599.  Please note that at least two examples, 56855 and 56856 demonstrate the bottom foot of the number ‘2’ and the very top of number ‘3’ at the serial prefix position, as shown in the below photograph.
There were at least two known pairs of identical serial number assignments in the 7th series standard ‘T.OZ’ production run.  One pair is owned by the founder of AE, serial number confirmation and photos are forthcoming.  The second pair of 56641 ingots is pictured below.
Ingot 56672 demonstrates a serial number stamping error, pictured below.  This example was initially assigned serial number 56671 then subsequently overstamped and corrected to 56672.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 8th series bar in ‘P’ serial prefix variation.  These bars are very similar in appearance to the common 10oz 10th series ‘P’ bars, yet this 8th series 5oz example had a surprisingly short production run for a later period Engelhard bar.  While the serial run of this series is projected from P00001 through P011800, we have noticed several sizeable gaps in serial ranges, and such gaps are utilized by other sizes and varieties of Engelhards, suggesting that the mintage of these may be far less than 10,000.  Early examples show a reverse “67” on the back of the bar.  Ingots P000073 and P000074 sold together via Mineral Exchange auction for $1,824.00 ($912.00 each) on May 18, 2025.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 1st series Canadian ingot in 5-digit serial variation.  This ingot’s 5-digit serial format, placement of weight/purity stampings and casting dimension are familiar to its 3rd series United States counterpart.  Canadian ingots across all weight class are typically observed in 6-digit serial format with purity stamping on left and weight class on right.  Based on this ingot’s dimension, we speculate Canada was initially provided with casting moulds used by the United States at that time for the first three series of Canadian ingots.  Ingot 89344 sold via eBay on March 11, 2016 for $809.00.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 3rd series Canadian ingot in 6-digit serial variation. This ingot includes a hallmark stamp unique to 1st and 2nd series ingots.  Based on this ingot’s dimension, we speculate Canada was initially provided with casting moulds used by the United States at that time for the first three series of Canadian ingots.  We have observed examples with both United States and Canadian serial number fonts in this series and believe the serial numbers were assigned with specific prefix for each run.  We also believe there was not a continuous production run for the entire range of serial numbers. Note the variations in spacing between purity and weight stamps.  The variety with wider spacing is more commonly observed than the variety with narrow spacing. Commentary: Canadian Prospector special release in proof finish.  Originally issued in a velvet box with Engelhard ‘Triangle’ logo on the inside, registered July 10, 1987, though seen in use as early as 1985.  Reverse design matches ‘Eagle’ logo that started in 1984, and research shows that the term ‘Canadian Prospector’ was trademarked by Engelhard in 1986. That suggests production between 1985-1986.  One example sold for $2,050.00 on November 7, 2024.

coins.notes:
Commentary: Engelhard produced this round to promote a new product line introduced by their Minerals and Chemicals Division. Everest was named after a thin electronic film paste, composed of a multi-layer hybrid electronic materials system of alternating layers of gold and silver, depending on the electrical characteristics. These rounds are often subject to milk-spots on the obverse, notably around the perimeter.  These rounds were produced in a proof-like finish, similar to the Prospector rounds listed above.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 1oz round, produced in proof variety in 1980.  Obverse depicts King Edward VII and reads “EDWARDVS VII DEI GRATIA REX IMPERATOR CANADA” which is Latin for “Edward VII – By the Grace of God, King and Emperor of Canada” originally issued in proof case with Certificate of Authenticity, pictured below.  Although the silver and gold set pictured includes the gold Engelhard round, it is accompanied by a Johnson and Matthey silver round.  Our high serial number of record is 6442, so it is possible the planned mintage of 10,000 units was ended early, similar to the set of four 100g London Hallmark Series ingots.

coins.notes:
Commentary: Interesting example of a 49.585oz platinum bar produced by Engelhard to fulfill one platinum contract.  Believed to have been produced in the late 1970’s or early 1980’s, this bar remained in the same family and in original wrap until its sale on April 13, 2010 for amount $91,525.00.  It is possible that this bar remains wrapped in the original wrapping to this day.  Will we ever get to see what the bar actually looks like?
Also possible.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 1oz example first produced in 1982 by Johnson Matthey. The reverse of these bars are not always aligned exactly the same. It seems that they are very similar, yet not always identical. Also, it appears that there was a slight redesign on the obverse, making the lettering and lines slightly thicker, somewhere between 297,000 to 639,000. This difference in font boldness may be seen in the comparison photo below.  It appears that when 999,999 was hit, an ‘R’ prefix was used with a 5-digit number, for example ‘R 01234′. Some examples were issued in plastic and includes assay certificates, pictured below.  Please note the varied serial number assignments, spanning from a five-digit and six-digit non prefix to the R-prefix examples noted in the next column.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 1oz example produced by Johnson Matthey, Canada.  Produced in honor of the 20 year anniversary of Canadian company Soqeum, founded in 1965.  Soquem was parent company of the Louvem, Niobec, Doyon and Seleine mining companies, as denoted on the reverse of this bar.  Niobec operated as the largest North American mining operation.  Seleine operated as a salt mine, Doyon as a gold mine and Louvem operated copper, zinc, silver and gold mines.  Appreciation to Dan S. for this discovery!
Sample Serials: 000235
Estimated Mintage: <100

coins.notes:
Commentary: 5 gram example produced in 1980 by Johnson Matthey, United States. Minted from bullion containing authentic treasure silver recovered from the wreck site of a Spanish treasure fleet, lost on July 30, 1715. Original order form mentions silver pendant and gold variations available as well. While some sources report 20k mintage, All Engelhard has not observed a serial higher than 7k, nor a gold variation. Original order form pictured below.


Johnson Matthey also produced this 5 gram variety in fine gold, pictured below.  We estimate mintage on the fine gold examples produced to be <250.

coins.notes:
Commentary: Produced from 1981-1985.  We believe there are three possible varieties to include a smooth ‘E’, a frosted ‘E’ and a third variety with a roughly frosted ‘E’ some of which demonstrate a proof-like finish.  Many FN and PR serial prefix examples demonstrate reverse variations of top right and bottom left hallmark border placement. Thanks to GOOB for these observations!  We believe the most rare and highest premium examples include the proof-like finish with frosted logo and reverse stamping variation.  One collector noted he purchased several FL prefix bars in plastic wrapper from Cypress Bank in Tampa, Florida, circa 1981.  Occasionally observed in serial placement error as seen on bar FJ67468, also pictured.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 1 KILO London ingot in portrait variation.  Note the large ‘M’ letter prefix on the example presented.  The ingot pictured was engraved for the 60th birthday of London Business Magnate Cyril Waterman, founder of Sherman & Waterman Associates Properties in London.  We don’t have confirmation of Waterman’s relationship with Charles Engelhard, but given this bar is one of two known large “M” portrait examples, it was likely designated with intent and significance.  M6295 was purchased from the Waterman estate on October 1, 2024 for 4,200.00GBP ($5,500.00US+/-).

coins.obverse_description: [100G] [Italic Boxed London Hallmark] [999]

coins.notes:
Commentary: ‘EI’ is the refiner’s mark [Engelhard International]. The leopard’s head is the London Assay Office hallmark. The seated Britannia traditionally indicates Britannia silver [.958], but in this case .999. The ‘U’ indicates 1969 [thanks, Ossi!]. Complete sets for $79.95 and individual ingots for $22.95 were originally advertised in the October 25, 1969 edition of The Saturday Review. These sets were not produced in subsequent years, as originally intended.  We believe far fewer sets were produced than the advertised 25,000 estimate. Additional details may be found at this LINK.
These ingots were originally offered via the sales advertisement below.  Our thanks to Adam Y. for the contribution!

coins.notes:
Commentary: 6th series 3oz ingot with serial stamp prefix anomaly.  We believe the serial number errors on these pieces resulted from the bottom half of the letter ‘X’ that was used as a filler for the lead position on the rotary stamp.  This leading filler ‘X’ slipped out of position just enough to leave an impression of the bottom half of the letter.  This theory is confirmed in the middle picture of 10oz bar 11948, listed on the 10oz Definitive page, where the bottom half of the same ‘8’ that was stamped on previous pieces had slipped back into the same position as the leading ‘X’ number to its left.  Some examples, such as the bottom one pictured, demonstrate an odd but vague ‘number shaped’ prefix similar to the similar 25oz class.

coins.notes:
Commentary: Produced in the late 1960’s, this is the earliest known variety of 1oz Engelhard production bar.  The examples we’ve observed demonstrate a faint ‘X8’ over-stamp to the left of serial number.  The serial number errors on these pieces resulted from part of the letter ‘X’ and number ‘8’ that were used as fillers for the lead position on the rotary stamp.  These leading fillers slipped out of position just enough to leave an impression of the bottom or top half of the letter and number, see our recent article ‘THE X8 FACTOR‘ for additional information.  Bar 10038 sold privately on August 12, 2016 for $2,500.00.  Bar 10088 sold privately on July 28, 2017 for $2,750.00.  Bar 10023 sold via eBay auction on May 2, 2021 for $2,555.99.  Additional information regarding this bar may be found at this LINK.

Commentary: 3rd series Australian bar, demonstrates a proof-like obverse surface finish. It is the most fully-refined of all variety and a keen eye will note the letter “G” is unique as it does not match that of the earlier bars. The frosted reverse demonstrates two full hallmarks in an ascending diagonal pattern, characteristic of these Australian examples.  One example sold on eBay on September 6, 2015 for $185.00.

coins.notes:
Commentary: 3oz weight class in extruded variation, known Engelhard hallmark.  Very rarely seen, perhaps because buyers and sellers are not aware they are Engelhard bars. Note that the ‘000080’ bar listed in our serial number column is correct, in that it lacks the ‘-‘ prefix common to other known examples.   Bar -000277 reached $1,175.00 [below reserve] in bidding via eBay auction on September 30,  2012.  Bar -000319 sold via eBay public auction for $1,050.99 on November 3, 2016.  Bar 001001 sold via eBay auction for $1,475.00 BIN on July 3, 2017 within hours of listing.   Bar -000409 sold via eBay auction for $2,375.00 on December 9, 2022.   Bar -000980 sold via Mineral Exchange auction for $2,585.00 on May 18, 2025.  Additional information may be found at this LINK.

coins.obverse_description: [7 OZ] [‘E’ Hallmark in Elongated Octagon] [999. FINE]

coins.notes:
Commentary: Produced in 1960’s and known  as ‘PENOLAS BAR’, from INDUSTRIAS PENOLAS, S.A. de C.V., of Mexico, the world’s leading refiner of primary silver.  Engelhard Industries purchased under annual contract .9995 silver bullion and grain for melt feed stock for alloying.  These are industrial ingots and vary in approximate 200oz weight class denomination.  These ingots were substantially consumed for industrial applications or remelted and very few exist today.  One example demonstrates a blatant over-stamp to correct the weight class stamp, shown below.  We have included original delivery correspondence from 1966 below.  Additional information may be found at this LINK.
